M3 · Bayesian random-effects hierarchy

Source: vignettes/m3-bayesian-hierarchy.Rmd
m3-bayesian-hierarchy.Rmd

Scope of this vignette

M1 motivated the empirical-Bayes deconvolution problem and M2 specified the log-spline prior on the mixing distribution gg. M3 composes those pieces into the full four-level Bayesian hierarchy that bayesEfron v0.1 fits. The vignette has four jobs. First, it states the hierarchy as a single equation block so the entire generative model is visible at one glance. Second, it draws the corresponding directed acyclic graph and walks through the conditional dependencies. Third, it derives the marginal of inferential interest — the per-site posterior p(θi∣𝛉̂)p(\theta_i \mid \hat{\boldsymbol\theta}) — and explains how the package’s generated quantities relate to that marginal and to functionals of gg. Fourth, it maps each of the thirteen named fields of fit$metadata back to the node in the DAG from which it originates.

Notation reminder

The notation is unchanged from M1 and M2. KK is the number of sites; θi\theta_i is the latent effect at site ii and θ̂i\hat\theta_i its observed estimate; σi\sigma_i is the known within-study standard error; gg is the discrete mixing distribution on a fixed length-LL grid {θ1,…,θL}\{\theta_1, \ldots, \theta_L\}; B∈ℝL×MB \in \mathbb{R}^{L \times M} is the fixed natural-cubic-spline basis evaluated at the grid; α∈ℝM\alpha \in \mathbb{R}^M is the spline-coefficient vector that determines gg through the softmax map of M2; and λ>0\lambda > 0 is the smoothness precision that governs the Gaussian conditional prior on α\alpha. No new symbols are introduced.

The four-level hierarchy

The full generative model for KK sites is, reading from the data upward:

Level 1 (likelihood):θ̂i∣θi∼𝒩(θi,σi2),i=1,…,K,Level 2 (latent effects):θi∣g∼iidg,Level 3 (mixing-distribution prior):gα(θl)=exp⁡(Bl⊤α)∑j=1Lexp⁡(Bj⊤α),l=1,…,L,Level 4 (hyperprior):α∣λ∼𝒩(𝟎,λ−1𝐈M),λ∼Cauchy+(0,5). \begin{aligned} \text{Level 1 (likelihood):} \quad \hat\theta_i \mid \theta_i &\;\sim\; \mathcal{N}\!\left(\theta_i, \sigma_i^2\right), \qquad i = 1, \ldots, K, \\ \text{Level 2 (latent effects):} \quad \theta_i \mid g &\;\stackrel{\mathrm{iid}}{\sim}\; g, \\ \text{Level 3 (mixing-distribution prior):} \quad g_{\alpha}(\theta_l) &\;=\; \frac{\exp\!\big(B_l^{\top}\alpha\big)} {\sum_{j=1}^{L} \exp\!\big(B_j^{\top}\alpha\big)}, \quad l = 1, \ldots, L, \\ \text{Level 4 (hyperprior):} \quad \alpha \mid \lambda &\;\sim\; \mathcal{N}\!\left(\mathbf{0},\, \lambda^{-1}\mathbf{I}_M\right), \\ \lambda &\;\sim\; \mathrm{Cauchy}^{+}(0, 5). \end{aligned}

Three points about the level structure are worth recording before discussing the DAG.

The first concerns the role of the grid. The grid 𝛉=(θ1,…,θL)\boldsymbol{\theta} = (\theta_1, \ldots, \theta_L) at Level 3 is fixed before sampling by make_efron_grid() (vignette M4) and is not a parameter of the model. The basis matrix BB is likewise fixed, being B=ns(𝛉;df=M,intercept=FALSE)B = \mathrm{ns}(\boldsymbol\theta;\ \mathrm{df} = M,\ \mathrm{intercept} = \mathrm{FALSE}). The Level 3 line above therefore specifies the deterministic map α↦g\alpha \mapsto g rather than a stochastic step: conditional on α\alpha, the prior gg is a function, not a draw.

The second is that the latent θi\theta_i at Level 2 are integrated out analytically. The per-site marginal likelihood, p(θ̂i∣g,σi)=∑j=1Lgj𝒩(θ̂i;θj,σi2), p(\hat\theta_i \mid g, \sigma_i) \;=\; \sum_{j=1}^{L} g_j\, \mathcal{N}\!\left(\hat\theta_i;\, \theta_j, \sigma_i^2\right), is a finite mixture of LL Gaussians, and it is this quantity that enters the Stan model block. The Hamiltonian Monte Carlo sampler runs over the unconstrained pair (α,λ)(\alpha, \lambda) in dimension M+1M + 1 (default 77) regardless of KK. The per-site posteriors on θi\theta_i are recovered in the generated-quantities block after sampling.

The third is the variance-versus-standard-deviation convention on α∣λ\alpha \mid \lambda. The display above writes the conditional prior in variance form, so each component of α\alpha has variance 1/λ1/\lambda and standard deviation λ−1/2\lambda^{-1/2}. The Stan source declares the same prior in standard-deviation form (alpha ~ normal(0, inv_sqrt(lambda))); the two are identical. The reference paper for the package’s prior writes the conditional prior in standard-deviation form, which the Stan source retains for paper-faithfulness. All three notations describe the same distribution.

DAG diagram

The directed acyclic graph corresponding to the hierarchy is shown below. Each node is a random or fixed quantity; each arrow encodes a conditional dependence of the head node on the tail node.

old_par <- par(no.readonly = TRUE)
on.exit(par(old_par), add = TRUE)
par(mar = c(0.5, 0.5, 0.5, 0.5))

plot.new()
plot.window(xlim = c(0, 10), ylim = c(0, 6.5), asp = 1)

draw_circle <- function(x, y, r = 0.45, lwd = 1.3) {
  ang <- seq(0, 2 * pi, length.out = 200)
  polygon(x + r * cos(ang), y + r * sin(ang), border = "black",
          col = "white", lwd = lwd)
}
draw_square <- function(x, y, r = 0.45, lwd = 1.3) {
  polygon(x + c(-r, r, r, -r), y + c(-r, -r, r, r),
          border = "black", col = "white", lwd = lwd)
}

# Coordinates: top to bottom.
x_lambda <- 2.0; y_lambda <- 5.7
x_alpha  <- 4.0; y_alpha  <- 5.7
x_g      <- 6.0; y_g      <- 5.7
x_B      <- 8.0; y_B      <- 5.7

x_theta  <- 5.0; y_theta  <- 3.3
x_hat    <- 5.0; y_hat    <- 1.1
x_sigma  <- 7.5; y_sigma  <- 1.1

# Plate enclosing the per-site sub-graph.
plate_x <- c(3.6, 8.4, 8.4, 3.6)
plate_y <- c(0.3, 0.3, 4.1, 4.1)
polygon(plate_x, plate_y, border = "grey55", lty = 2, lwd = 1)
text(8.25, 0.55, expression(italic(i) == 1 * "," ~ ldots * "," ~ italic(K)),
     cex = 0.85, col = "grey35", adj = c(1, 0))

# Nodes.
draw_circle(x_lambda, y_lambda)
text(x_lambda, y_lambda, expression(lambda), cex = 1.25)

draw_circle(x_alpha,  y_alpha)
text(x_alpha,  y_alpha,  expression(alpha),  cex = 1.25)

draw_circle(x_g, y_g)
text(x_g, y_g, expression(italic(g)), cex = 1.25)

draw_square(x_B, y_B)
text(x_B, y_B, expression(italic(B)), cex = 1.1)

draw_circle(x_theta, y_theta)
text(x_theta, y_theta, expression(theta[italic(i)]), cex = 1.1)

draw_circle(x_hat, y_hat)
text(x_hat, y_hat, expression(hat(theta)[italic(i)]), cex = 1.1)

draw_square(x_sigma, y_sigma)
text(x_sigma, y_sigma, expression(sigma[italic(i)]), cex = 1.1)

# Arrows.
arrow_between <- function(x0, y0, x1, y1, r = 0.45, length = 0.10) {
  dx <- x1 - x0; dy <- y1 - y0; d <- sqrt(dx^2 + dy^2)
  ux <- dx / d;  uy <- dy / d
  arrows(x0 + r * ux, y0 + r * uy, x1 - r * ux, y1 - r * uy,
         length = length, angle = 22, lwd = 1.2, col = "black")
}
arrow_between(x_lambda, y_lambda, x_alpha, y_alpha)
arrow_between(x_alpha,  y_alpha,  x_g,     y_g)
arrow_between(x_B,      y_B,      x_g,     y_g)
arrow_between(x_g,      y_g,      x_theta, y_theta)
arrow_between(x_theta,  y_theta,  x_hat,   y_hat)
arrow_between(x_sigma,  y_sigma,  x_hat,   y_hat)

# Legend.
legend(
  "topleft", bty = "n", cex = 0.8,
  legend = c("random quantity", "fixed input"),
  pch    = c(1, 0), pt.cex = 1.3
)

Directed acyclic graph of the four-level Bayesian random-effects hierarchy fit by bayesEfron. The smoothness precision lambda controls the Gaussian conditional prior on the spline coefficients alpha; alpha and the fixed basis B together determine the discrete mixing distribution g; latent site effects theta_i are drawn iid from g; observed estimates hat_theta_i are Gaussian around theta_i with known standard errors sigma_i. Random quantities are shown as circles, fixed inputs as squares. The plate over i = 1, …, K indicates K independent replicates of the per-site sub-graph.

The diagram resolves the conditional dependence structure of the joint distribution. The hyperprior on λ\lambda controls the conditional Gaussian prior on α\alpha; the spline coefficients α\alpha, together with the fixed basis BB, deterministically produce the discrete prior gg; iid draws from gg produce the latent site effects θi\theta_i; and each observed estimate θ̂i\hat\theta_i is Gaussian around its latent counterpart θi\theta_i with the known standard error σi\sigma_i. The plate captures the conditional independence of the KK per-site sub-graphs given the upper-level quantities (λ,α,g)(\lambda, \alpha, g): θi\theta_i and θi′\theta_{i'} are conditionally independent given gg, and θ̂i\hat\theta_i and θ̂i′\hat\theta_{i'} are conditionally independent given (θi,θi′)(\theta_i, \theta_{i'}) and the corresponding standard errors. Random quantities are circles; fixed inputs (σi\sigma_i supplied by the user, BB constructed by make_efron_grid()) are squares. The graph is acyclic and the joint factorises as p(λ)p(α∣λ)∏ip(θi∣g)p(θ̂i∣θi,σi)p(\lambda)\, p(\alpha \mid \lambda)\, \prod_i p(\theta_i \mid g)\, p(\hat\theta_i \mid \theta_i, \sigma_i).

The marginal of inferential interest

The hierarchy is fit so that the per-site posterior p(θi∣𝛉̂)p(\theta_i \mid \hat{\boldsymbol\theta}) — and its functionals — can be summarised. At fixed gg the per-site posterior factorises by Bayes’s rule applied to the convolution: p(θi∣θ̂i,σi,g)=p(θ̂i∣θi,σi)g(θi)p(θ̂i∣σi,g), p(\theta_i \mid \hat\theta_i, \sigma_i, g) \;=\; \frac{p(\hat\theta_i \mid \theta_i, \sigma_i)\; g(\theta_i)} {p(\hat\theta_i \mid \sigma_i, g)}, with denominator the per-site marginal likelihood p(θ̂i∣σi,g)=∑jgj𝒩(θ̂i;θj,σi2)p(\hat\theta_i \mid \sigma_i, g) = \sum_j g_j\, \mathcal{N}(\hat\theta_i; \theta_j, \sigma_i^2) already encountered above. On the grid this specialises to a discrete posterior on {θ1,…,θL}\{\theta_1, \ldots, \theta_L\} with weights wij∝exp⁡{log⁡gj+log⁡𝒩(θ̂i;θj,σi2)},∑jwij=1. w_{ij} \;\propto\; \exp\!\big\{\log g_j + \log \mathcal{N}(\hat\theta_i;\, \theta_j, \sigma_i^2)\big\}, \qquad \sum_j w_{ij} = 1. The Stan source computes wijw_{ij} in the generated-quantities block using the max-shift normalisation discussed in M5 to prevent underflow when individual log-components differ by tens of units.

The crucial subtlety is that the package does not report p(θi∣θ̂i,σi,g)p(\theta_i \mid \hat\theta_i, \sigma_i, g) at a fixed gg. It reports the marginal posterior p(θi∣𝛉̂)=∫p(θi∣θ̂i,σi,g(α))p(α,λ∣𝛉̂)dαdλ, p(\theta_i \mid \hat{\boldsymbol\theta}) \;=\; \int p(\theta_i \mid \hat\theta_i, \sigma_i, g(\alpha))\; p(\alpha, \lambda \mid \hat{\boldsymbol\theta})\, \mathrm{d}\alpha\,\mathrm{d}\lambda, which integrates the at-fixed-gg posterior over the joint posterior of (α,λ)(\alpha, \lambda). The integration is performed Monte Carlo via the HMC draws: at each iteration s=1,…,Ss = 1, \ldots, S the sampler produces (α(s),λ(s))(\alpha^{(s)}, \lambda^{(s)}), the deterministic map yields g(s)=softmax(Bα(s))g^{(s)} = \mathrm{softmax}(B \alpha^{(s)}), the per-site weights wij(s)w_{ij}^{(s)} are recomputed at g(s)g^{(s)}, and the per-site posterior summaries (mean, standard deviation, MAP, replicate) are computed at that iteration’s g(s)g^{(s)}. Posterior summaries on θi\theta_i are then Monte Carlo averages or quantiles over the SS iterations. The integration over (α,λ)(\alpha, \lambda) is therefore implicit in the across-iteration aggregation.

The shrinkage interpretation of the per-site posterior follows from the weights formula. Each wijw_{ij} is the product of the prior mass gjg_j at grid point θj\theta_j and the Gaussian sampling kernel 𝒩(θ̂i;θj,σi2)\mathcal{N}(\hat\theta_i; \theta_j, \sigma_i^2), normalised across jj. When σi\sigma_i is small the kernel is sharp at θ̂i\hat\theta_i and the weights concentrate near grid points close to θ̂i\hat\theta_i, so the posterior on θi\theta_i sits close to θ̂i\hat\theta_i regardless of gg. When σi\sigma_i is large the kernel is broad and the prior gg dominates the weights, so the posterior on θi\theta_i is pulled toward the mass of gg. The strength of shrinkage toward gg is therefore site-specific and continuous in σi\sigma_i, which is the standard Robbins-Efron empirical-Bayes shrinkage pattern adapted to a heteroscedastic sampling design.

Functionals of gg

The package also reports posterior summaries of two functionals of gg: its mean and its variance. At a single iteration ss with g(s)=softmax(Bα(s))g^{(s)} = \mathrm{softmax}(B \alpha^{(s)}), these functionals are deterministic sums on the grid: 𝔼g(s)[θ]=∑l=1Lθlgl(s),Varg(s)[θ]=∑l=1L(θl−𝔼g(s)[θ])2gl(s). \mathbb{E}_{g^{(s)}}[\theta] \;=\; \sum_{l=1}^{L} \theta_l\, g_l^{(s)}, \qquad \mathrm{Var}_{g^{(s)}}[\theta] \;=\; \sum_{l=1}^{L} \big(\theta_l - \mathbb{E}_{g^{(s)}}[\theta]\big)^2\, g_l^{(s)}. The standard deviation is Varg(s)[θ]\sqrt{\mathrm{Var}_{g^{(s)}}[\theta]}. Stan computes these as the generated quantities mean_g, var_g, and sd_g, respectively, using the two-pass form 𝔼[θ2]−𝔼[θ]2\mathbb{E}[\theta^2] - \mathbb{E}[\theta]^2 on the grid (vignette M5 documents the numerical discipline).

The metadata fields mean_g_summary, var_g_summary, and sd_g_summary carry posterior summaries of these draw-wise quantities across the SS HMC iterations. A posterior median of mean_g_summary is therefore the median across iterations of ∑lθlgl(s)\sum_l \theta_l\, g_l^{(s)} — an expectation under the posterior of gg, not a property of any single gg-curve. Two facts about this construction deserve emphasis.

First, the package does not return a posterior summary of an arbitrary functional T(g)T(g). The three functionals it does return (the first moment, the variance, and the standard deviation) are sufficient for the calibration claims the package makes and parsimonious enough to fit in the 13-field metadata contract; richer functionals (quantiles of gg, tail probabilities, density values at user-specified anchors) are available through fit$draws for users willing to compute them externally.

Second, the integration over the joint posterior of (α,λ)(\alpha, \lambda) is the load-bearing distinction between the package’s reports of gg and a fixed-gg plug-in. The plug-in summaries from an empirical-Bayes estimator condition on ĝ=gα̂\hat g = g_{\hat\alpha} as if it were known; the posterior summaries here propagate the uncertainty in (α,λ)(\alpha, \lambda) into every functional of gg that the package returns.

The 13-field metadata contract from the hierarchy perspective

The fit$metadata list has exactly thirteen named fields and four durable attributes. The named fields are model_family, grid_method, seed, cmdstan_version, stan_file_sha256, data_list, runtime_seconds, mean_g_summary, var_g_summary, theta_summary, theta_rep_draws, effective_params_summary, and log_marginal_likelihood_summary. The durable attributes are sd_g_summary, diagnostics, diagnostic_skipped, and sampler_diagnostics_failed. The provenance from the DAG nodes is direct.

Two fields are per-site posterior summaries on θi\theta_i, derived from the marginal p(θi∣𝛉̂)p(\theta_i \mid \hat{\boldsymbol\theta}) of the Level 1 + Level 2 sub-graph after integration over (α,λ)(\alpha, \lambda):

  • theta_summary — posterior point summaries (mean, standard deviation, posterior intervals) of θi\theta_i for each i=1,…,Ki = 1, \ldots, K.
  • theta_rep_draws — full draws of the posterior predictive replicate θirep\theta_i^{\mathrm{rep}} (see next section), retained because the release-blocking coverage diagnostic is built from across-draw quantiles of these replicates rather than from across-draw quantiles of the posterior mean estimator.

Two named fields plus one attribute are posterior summaries of functionals of gg, derived from the Level 3 sub-graph after integration over (α,λ)(\alpha, \lambda):

  • mean_g_summary — posterior summary of 𝔼g[θ]\mathbb{E}_g[\theta] (named field).
  • var_g_summary — posterior summary of Varg[θ]\mathrm{Var}_g[\theta] (named field).
  • sd_g_summary — posterior summary of SDg[θ]\mathrm{SD}_g[\theta], stored as a durable attribute of fit$metadata. The attribute layout is documented on ?bayes_efron_fit.

Two fields are model-quality diagnostics on the joint posterior:

  • effective_params_summary — posterior summary of the diagnostic effective-parameter count peff=K−∑iVar[θi∣θ̂i,g]/σi2p_{\mathrm{eff}} = K - \sum_i \mathrm{Var}[\theta_i \mid \hat\theta_i, g] / \sigma_i^2. The quantity is exposed for inspection only; vignette M5 discusses the σi→0\sigma_i \to 0 amplification that limits its usefulness as a model-comparison statistic.
  • log_marginal_likelihood_summary — posterior summary of ∑ilog⁡p(θ̂i∣g,σi)\sum_i \log p(\hat\theta_i \mid g, \sigma_i), the data’s log-marginal under the per-iteration gg. Future versions (v0.2+) will build a publication-ready LOO-CV facility on top of this quantity.

Seven fields are reproducibility and bookkeeping metadata rather than nodes in the DAG:

  • model_family — the model identifier ("RE" for v0.1).
  • grid_method — the grid recipe used to construct 𝛉\boldsymbol\theta and BB (vignette M4).
  • data_list — the seven-field Stan data block that was actually sent to CmdStan (K, theta_hat, sigma, L, grid, M, B).
  • runtime_seconds — sampler wall-clock.
  • seed, cmdstan_version, stan_file_sha256 — the integer seed used for the run (auto-generated if seed = NULL at the call site), the CmdStan version string, and the SHA-256 of the byte-locked Stan source. Together with data_list and a matching toolchain environment, these support practical re-play of the fit.

The remaining three durable attributes (diagnostics, diagnostic_skipped, sampler_diagnostics_failed) surface the sampler-diagnostic findings; they are consumed by summary() and diagnose() rather than read directly. Together with sd_g_summary, they make up the four attribute payloads stated at the top of this section.

Posterior predictive replicates

The release-blocking interval estimand is the posterior predictive replicate θirep\theta_i^{\mathrm{rep}}. At each MCMC iteration ss, with g(s)g^{(s)} available and per-site weights wij(s)w_{ij}^{(s)} computed, θirep\theta_i^{\mathrm{rep}} is drawn from the per-site posterior on the grid: θirep,(s)∼Categorical𝛉(𝐰(i,s)),wij(s)∝exp⁡{log⁡gj(s)+log⁡𝒩(θ̂i;θj,σi2)}. \theta_i^{\mathrm{rep}, (s)} \;\sim\; \mathrm{Categorical}_{\boldsymbol\theta}\!\big(\mathbf{w}^{(i, s)}\big), \qquad w_{ij}^{(s)} \propto \exp\!\big\{\log g_j^{(s)} + \log \mathcal{N}(\hat\theta_i;\, \theta_j, \sigma_i^2)\big\}. This is a draw from the distribution p(θ∣θ̂i,σi,g(s))∝g(s)(θ)p(θ̂i∣θ,σi)p(\theta \mid \hat\theta_i, \sigma_i, g^{(s)}) \propto g^{(s)}(\theta)\, p(\hat\theta_i \mid \theta, \sigma_i) on the grid, for fixed site ii and at the current iteration’s g(s)g^{(s)}.

The distinction from the per-iteration posterior mean θimean,(s)=∑jwij(s)θj\theta_i^{\mathrm{mean}, (s)} = \sum_j w_{ij}^{(s)}\, \theta_j is load-bearing. Quantiles of theta_mean across MCMC iterations give a credible interval for the estimator; they omit the conditional posterior dispersion of θi\theta_i around its mean at each iteration. Quantiles of theta_rep across MCMC iterations integrate over both the across-iteration uncertainty in (α,λ,g)(\alpha, \lambda, g) and the within-iteration posterior on θi\theta_i, giving a calibrated parameter-uncertainty interval. The package stores the full draws as theta_rep_draws precisely so that the calibrated intervals can be recomputed from the fit object without re-sampling. Vignette M6 reports the empirical coverage of these intervals on the locked Lee-Sui benchmark.

Reading map

This vignette stated the hierarchy, diagrammed it, derived the marginal and the functionals of gg that the package returns, and mapped the thirteen metadata fields back to the DAG. The remaining methodological vignettes carry the construction into implementation and verification.

  • M4 — Grid construction. The four grid recipes exposed by make_efron_grid() (paper_realdata, paper_simulation, paper_sensitivity, kl_target_experimental), the construction of the basis matrix BB, the rank-postcondition guard against identifiability failure, and the trade-offs between grid width and resolution.
  • M5 — Stan model and cache. The byte-locked Stan source inst/stan/efron_re.stan, the ten numerical-stability disciplines (log_softmax, log_sum_exp, max-shift normalisation, two-pass variance), and the two-tier cache that prevents redundant compilation.
  • M6 — Verification and calibration. End-to-end coverage verification on the locked benchmark fixtures, the release-blocking acceptance band on the per-site replicate intervals ([0.87,0.92][0.87, 0.92] at nominal 0.900.90), and the diagnostic surface of the fitted object.

A reader who has followed M1–M3 now has the complete model specification and a clear picture of the inferential payload fit$metadata carries; M4–M6 close the loop from specification to implementation and verified calibration.

On this page

Developed by JoonHo Lee.

Site built with pkgdown 2.2.0.
